bins.name: State Jurisdiction Must Be Preserved
bins.desc: Southern Companies warn that FERC lacks authority to override state jurisdiction over retail service and bundled transmission, and that any national standard imposing load interconnection requirements on vertically-integrated states would face litigation. They urge the Commission to proceed only where it has clear legal footing and to avoid displacing state-supervised processes.
quotes: The vertically integrated market structure in Georgia allows the GPSC to oversee every facet of this load growth and adjust to market conditions as they emerge.

establishing a national standard would require those regions that are already successfully interconnecting large loads to seek a variance through the Commission's compliance process, which could be time-consuming, burdensome, and litigious, ultimately slowing interconnections rather than accelerating the goals of the ANOPR.
the Commission can and should proceed in a manner that avoids unnecessary disputes over jurisdiction concerning FERC-jurisdictional service to retail customers.

traditional state jurisdiction over retail utility service should not be overridden, a particularly important principle in regions where the states have retained and exercised jurisdiction over transmission as part of bundled retail service.
A narrower approach—focused on guidance, best practices, or minimum principles—offers a more durable, timely, and legally sustainable path.

Southern Companies argued that the Commission could achieve the objectives of the ANOPR by using lessons learned from this proceeding to establish a list of national best practices for large load interconnections and adopt a policy statement or similar vehicle to encourage the use of these best practices.

bins.name: Flexibility Over Uniform Mandates
bins.desc: The filing argues that a prescriptive national rule would burden regions already achieving timely interconnections, requiring them to seek compliance variances and inviting litigation. A narrower approach built on guidance, best practices, or minimum principles is presented as more durable and less disruptive to successful regional models.
quotes: many parties have expressed concern that progress already made could be undermined if the Commission were to enact an overly prescriptive final rule.
FERC should consider adopting broad principles that allow for innovation and regional flexibility, rather than narrow prescriptions.

establishing a national standard would require those regions that are already successfully interconnecting large loads to seek a variance through the Commission's compliance process, which could be time-consuming, burdensome, and litigious, ultimately slowing interconnections rather than accelerating the goals of the ANOPR.
A narrower approach—focused on guidance, best practices, or minimum principles—offers a more durable, timely, and legally sustainable path.

Southern Companies argued that the Commission could achieve the objectives of the ANOPR by using lessons learned from this proceeding to establish a list of national best practices for large load interconnections and adopt a policy statement or similar vehicle to encourage the use of these best practices.

bins.name: Integrated Planning Addresses Power Supply
bins.desc: Southern Companies contend that interconnection reform alone cannot solve the AI boom's power supply challenge. They highlight their integrated resource planning process, which evaluates generation, fuel, and transmission together under state oversight, as the mechanism that has kept the Southeast free from resource adequacy crises seen elsewhere.
quotes: Transmission interconnection reform alone cannot ensure that adequate generating capacity, fuel supply, and supporting infrastructure will exist to serve rapidly growing large loads.

the Commission should be mindful, while considering load interconnection reforms, not to create obstacles to integrated resource planning processes that already consider power supply, fuel, and transmission together, and which have the State regulatory support needed to build the generation resources to support AI dominance.

bins.name: Large-Load Customers Bear Full Costs
bins.desc: The filing defends Georgia Power's approach of requiring large-load customers to pay the full costs of serving them, including minimum bill requirements and termination provisions, as a model that protects existing retail customers and limits stranded-investment risk.
quotes: Georgia Power has worked closely with the GPSC to balance the need for rapid response to large loads with a desire to protect existing retail customers from bearing any undue costs.
large load customers pay the full costs needed to serve them.

bins.name: Southeast Already Achieving Goals
bins.desc: Southern Companies document concrete Southeast results, including 74% data center load growth from 2022 to 2025, 7 GW of contracted large-load service, and 4 GW of added generation, to argue that the region's vertically-integrated model is already delivering the outcomes the ANOPR seeks.
quotes: Southern Companies have been able to plan for and support 74% growth in data center load from 2022-2025.
many parties have expressed concern that progress already made could be undermined if the Commission were to enact an overly prescriptive final rule.
The vertically integrated market structure in Georgia allows the GPSC to oversee every facet of this load growth and adjust to market conditions as they emerge.

bins.name: IRP Links Generation Fuel and Transmission
bins.desc: The filing argues that interconnection rulemaking must not disrupt integrated resource planning, which coordinates generation additions, fuel supply, and transmission upgrades under state authority. This holistic approach is credited with avoiding the resource adequacy problems seen in PJM and other regions.
quotes: Transmission interconnection reform alone cannot ensure that adequate generating capacity, fuel supply, and supporting infrastructure will exist to serve rapidly growing large loads.

the Commission should be mindful, while considering load interconnection reforms, not to create obstacles to integrated resource planning processes that already consider power supply, fuel, and transmission together, and which have the State regulatory support needed to build the generation resources to support AI dominance.

bins.name: Existing Customers Protected From Large-Load Costs
bins.desc: Southern Companies rebut criticism from Southeast public interest organizations by explaining that Georgia Power's contract terms, including minimum bills and termination provisions, ensure that new large-load customers bear the full costs of their interconnection and service, shielding existing retail customers.
quotes: Georgia Power has worked closely with the GPSC to balance the need for rapid response to large loads with a desire to protect existing retail customers from bearing any undue costs.
large load customers pay the full costs needed to serve them.
